generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance HeatbedController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Embedded software controller on Raspberry Pi that monitors and actuates the heatbed and fan based on predefined control logic for real-time temperature regulation"
]

instance HeatbedActuator : DTDFVocab:Actuator [
    base:desc "Primary heating element within the insulated styrofoam incubator box that provides controlled thermal energy distribution"
]

instance FanActuator : DTDFVocab:Actuator [
    base:desc "Mechanical fan within the incubator that actively distributes generated heat uniformly throughout the insulated container"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance InternalTemperatureSensor1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire Digital Thermometer measuring internal temperature of the incubator's core"
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData
]

instance InternalTemperatureSensor2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire Digital Thermometer measuring secondary internal temperature within the insulated container"
    DTDFVocab:producedData InternalTemperatureData
]

instance ExternalTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision DS18S20 1-Wire Digital Thermometer measuring ambient external temperature outside the styrofoam box"
    DTDFVocab:producedData ExternalTemperatureData
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance InternalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom InternalTemperatureSensor1, InternalTemperatureSensor2
    base:desc "Raw temperature readings (in Celsius) from internal sensors transmitted via 1-Wire protocol"
]

instance ExternalTemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ExternalTemperatureSensor
    base:desc "Raw temperature readings (in Celsius) from external sensor transmitted via 1-Wire protocol"
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance TwoParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Basic two-parameter thermal model for temperature prediction with minimal complexity representing incubator system dynamics"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo CalibrationAlgorithmsEnabler
]

instance FourParameterThermalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Enhanced four-parameter thermal model capturing thermal inertia of heatbed for more accurate temperature prediction"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo CalibrationAlgorithmsEnabler, ExperimentalDataEnabler
]

instance TemperatureMeasurementData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed temperature measurement data (in Celsius) from all sensors, refined through calibration algorithms"
    DTDFVocab:fromData InternalTemperatureData, ExternalTemperatureData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEnabler, StateEstimationEnabler
]

instance CalibrationParametersData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Dynamic model parameters derived from calibration algorithms for real-time model updates"
    DTDFVocab:fromData TemperatureMeasurementData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FourParameterThermalModel
]

// C11: Enablers
instance RabbitMQServer : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Real-time data messaging middleware facilitating aggregation and distribution of sensor data within the DT infrastructure"
]

instance CalibrationAlgorithmsEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Python-based calibration algorithms that update model parameters using sensor feedback for improved cyber-physical alignment"
    DTDFVocab:enables AutonomousCalibrationService
]

instance SimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SciPy-based simulation framework executing differential equation models for temperature trend forecasting"
    DTDFVocab:enables PredictiveAnalysisService
]

instance StateEstimationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "State estimation algorithms processing real-time sensor data for accurate state tracking vs target temperature deviations"
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeMonitoringService
]

instance ExperimentalDataEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Data repository storing experimental validation results from incubator case studies for model refinement"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo FourParameterThermalModel
]

instance RaspberryPiController : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Central controller interfacing with sensors and actuators via 1-Wire protocol, executing closed-loop control logic"
    DTDFVocab:enables PhysicalFeedbackService
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous acquisition of sensor data (internal/external temperatures) feeding into state estimation algorithms for actual vs target temperature tracking"
    DTDFVocab:provides TemperatureDeviationInsight, AlertThresholdAction
]

instance PredictiveAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Simulation-based forecasting of temperature trends using calibrated thermal models for proactive control adjustments"
    DTDFVocab:provides TemperatureTrendInsight, ParameterAdjustmentAction
]

instance AutonomousCalibrationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Algorithmic recalibration of DT models (thermal transfer coefficients) using feedback from physical sensors"
    DTDFVocab:provides ModelParameterUpdateAction
]

instance SafetyViolationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Violation detection mechanisms triggering human or automated interventions when physical system deviates from safe operating conditions"
    DTDFVocab:provides InterventionAction
]

instance ReconfigurableControlService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dynamic adaptation of control parameters (fan speed, heatbed power) to accommodate physical environment changes"
    DTDFVocab:provides ParameterAdjustmentAction
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance TemperatureDeviationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time comparison between actual and target temperatures from sensor data, indicating current operating conditions"
]

instance TemperatureTrendInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predicted temperature trends based on simulation models, showing future temperature behavior under current conditions"
]

instance ModelParameterUpdateAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automatic update of model parameters using calibration algorithms to improve cyber-physical alignment"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance AlertThresholdAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Triggered when temperature exceeds predefined safety thresholds, initiating intervention procedures"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance InterventionAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated or manual intervention actions to correct physical system deviations from safe operating conditions"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ParameterAdjustmentAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Dynamic adjustment of control parameters (fan speed, heatbed power) based on reconfigurable cyber model"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C1: Environment
instance IncubatorEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains HeatbedActuator, FanActuator, InternalTemperatureSensor1, InternalTemperatureSensor2, ExternalTemperatureSensor, RaspberryPiController
]

instance StyrofoamInsulationLayer : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains InternalTemperatureSensor1, InternalTemperatureSensor2
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
  The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the DT employs a **bang-bang controller** to regulate the incubator's temperature by sending control commands via **GPIO (General-Purpose Input/Output) connectors** on the Raspberry Pi. Command types include binary on/off signals for the heatbed and fan, validated through a **safe protocol** integrated into the low-level driver to ensure system reliability.

  Feedback mechanisms involve real-time monitoring of temperature deviations, triggering adjustments to the physical system (e.g., activating/deactivating heating or ventilation). There is no explicit mention of PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) or detailed command validation/authentication protocols beyond the safe protocol in the low-level driver.
 "
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin for the incubator system synchronizes data collection and model updates with the physical twin's temporal dynamics. Temperature sensors (internal and external) are read **periodically** to monitor the system's thermal state, though exact synchronization frequencies are not specified in the documents. The physical system's thermal response—such as the fan's air distribution taking **2 seconds** to fully circulate—implies a **sub-minute** temporal granularity requirement for real-time monitoring and control. The Raspberry Pi acts as the central controller, processing sensor data and executing model-driven decisions, but no explicit latency or real-time constraints (e.g., millisecond-to-second range) are quantified beyond the implied need for responsiveness to thermal changes. The digital twin’s temporal granularity aligns with the physical system’s dynamics, where calibration and monitoring must adapt to transient thermal variations."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **centralized cyber-physical system (CPS) architecture** (as illustrated in Fig. 1), where the DT integrates **three core components**:
1. **Data**: Collected via temperature sensors (internal/external) and distributed through the Raspberry Pi’s control loop.
2. **Models**: Two distinct models are built with calibration methods (Section 3), representing the **CPS (cyber-physical system) and its environment** (e.g., thermal dynamics of the styrofoam box). These models are updated via **online calibration algorithms** to align with real-time sensor data.
3. **Algorithms**: Includes **state estimators, fault detectors, and optimizers** (e.g., supervisory controllers) to ensure safe and optimal operation. The DT orchestrates these via a **closed-loop control framework**, where violations trigger human/algorithm interventions (e.g., adjusting heatbed/fan settings).

The orchestration pattern is **event-driven**, with calibration and monitoring loops executing in parallel. Resource management is implicit but tied to the Raspberry Pi’s computational capacity, ensuring real-time performance for the DT’s services.
"
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle for the Incubator case study, as implied by the documents, follows a structured yet abstracted framework where models evolve alongside the Physical Twin (PT). Key stages include:

1. **Conceptualization Phase**: Development begins with defining the DT’s purpose—specifically, to regulate incubator temperature and optimize performance. Models are initially abstract, capturing basic structural and functional aspects (e.g., CAD-like representations of the styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and sensors) but excluding dynamic behavior (e.g., forces acting on the system).

2. **Modeling Phase**: Two specialized models are built using calibration methods to refine cyber-physical representations. These models incorporate:
   - **Cyber Models**: Parameterized abstractions of the CPS (Cyber-Physical System) and its environment (e.g., temperature dynamics, sensor noise, thermal coupling).
   - **Physics Models**: Differential-equation-based simulations (e.g., thermal transfer, fan airflow) to predict PT behavior under varying conditions. The documents note that accurate models may require trade-offs between fidelity and computational efficiency (e.g., simulation speed).

3. **Integration Phase**: The DT integrates **data streams** (collected via sensors: 3 internal + 1 external temperature sensors) with calibrated models and algorithms (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) into a unified framework. A Raspberry Pi acts as the control hub, interfacing with the PT’s hardware (e.g., heatbed, fan) and DT’s cyber components.

4. **Operational Phase**: Post-deployment, the DT continuously updates models via calibration algorithms (e.g., parameter estimation) using real-time sensor data. This loop enables:
   - **Reconfigurability**: Adaptive adjustments to the PT (e.g., adjusting fan speed or heatbed output) to maintain target temperature despite environmental changes.
   - **Safety Monitoring**: Algorithms detect deviations (e.g., sensor anomalies, thermal drift) and trigger corrective actions (human or automated).

**Lifecycle Governance**: While not explicitly detailed, the documents imply a **modular approach** where models are version-controlled (e.g., via GitHub repository [2]) and evolve incrementally. Future work (Section 5) suggests iterative refinement based on experimental results (Section 4), but no explicit lifecycle governance tools (e.g., CMMI, DevOps pipelines) are mentioned.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
**Model Fidelity Requirements**:
- The **four-parameter model** is prioritized for capturing thermal inertia due to its superior alignment with real-world incubator dynamics, validated through experimental comparisons with the two-parameter model.

**Validation and Verification Methods**:
- Real-world experiments were conducted to validate the models, demonstrating that the four-parameter model provides more accurate temperature predictions for the incubator's physical system.
- The DT integrates **calibration algorithms** to iteratively update model parameters based on discrepancies between simulated and real-time sensor data, ensuring continuous validity.

**Uncertainty Quantification**:
- The primary uncertainty source is attributed to the model's parameter estimation, particularly in thermal inertia representation, though no explicit quantification methods (e.g., Monte Carlo simulations) are detailed in the documents.

**Error Detection and Correction**:
- The system relies on real-time sensor feedback to detect deviations between predicted and actual temperatures, triggering recalibration of the four-parameter model to correct errors dynamically.
- No explicit mention of hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing, Kalman filters, or ISO 10504 compliance is present in the provided documents.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
  The **technical network infrastructure** for the DT relies on:
  - **1-Wire bus** for sensor communication (low-speed, low-power, reliable for temperature monitoring).
  - **RabbitMQ server** deployed on the Raspberry Pi for message broker synchronization, using **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** for inter-process communication.
  No explicit details on bandwidth, latency, or security protocols (e.g., TLS) are provided in the documents. The system does not mention redundancy or failover mechanisms for network communication.
 "
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
  The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the DT is **on-premise**, implemented on a **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** as the primary computational node. The system is deployed as a **single-node, edge-based solution** with no cloud or distributed deployment mentioned. Computational requirements include embedded processing for sensor data acquisition and control logic. Storage is implied to be minimal (likely SD card-based) due to the lightweight nature of the incubator case study. The system is **not explicitly scalable**, and reliability is ensured through the use of a single, dedicated hardware node (Raspberry Pi) with no redundancy or high availability guarantees specified.
  "
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
A Digital Twin (DT) system in the provided context integrates **calibration algorithms** to dynamically update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment using real-time sensor data from the Physical Twin (PT). This process ensures that the DT's models reflect the current operational state of the PT, enabling accurate monitoring of system performance.

The DT employs **monitoring mechanisms** to detect deviations from optimal or safe operating conditions. These monitors leverage the calibrated models and data streams to assess whether the PT is functioning within predefined safety and performance boundaries. When violations are identified, the system may trigger **fail-safe protocols** or interventions via human operators or automated algorithms to mitigate risks.

While the documents do not explicitly mention cybersecurity protocols, authentication systems, or formal risk assessment methodologies, the DT framework inherently relies on:
- **Data integrity verification** through continuous model recalibration with sensor inputs.
- **Safety-critical system considerations** via real-time monitoring and automated response mechanisms (e.g., state estimators, fault detectors).
- **Environmental and operational constraints** embedded in the DT’s simulation models to ensure compliance with safety standards during dynamic analysis.
"
]
